title: Huge ultrafast spin Seebeck effect mediated by laser-excited superdiffusive magnon currents
authors: Luca Mikadze, Peter M. Oppeneer, Markus Weißenhofer
abstract: Subpicosecond laser excitation of ferromagnetic metals induces strongly nonequilibrium dynamics involving scattering and transport of electrons, phonons, and magnons. Widely used theoretical approaches, such as the three-temperature model and diffusion equations, are ill-suited to capture these processes on ultrafast timescales. Here, we present an ab initio-parameterized microscopic framework that incorporates nonthermal magnon scattering and transport via the quantum Boltzmann equation. We apply this approach to simulate ultrafast laser-induced demagnetization in bcc Fe films. The model predicts an ultrafast spin Seebeck effect, characterized by a strong burst of fast-moving magnonic spin current reaching technologically relevant amplitudes. Furthermore, we identify a superdiffusive transport regime: a crossover from initially ballistic magnon transport to a diffusive regime at later times. To connect our theoretical predictions to experimentally accessible observables, we calculate the magneto-optical Kerr angles resulting from the predicted depth-resolved magnetization profiles. Our framework provides a route to describe ultrafast nonthermal magnon transport beyond diffusive models and will aid in the design and interpretation of time-resolved spin-transport experiments.
summary: The paper develops a new theoretical framework to describe how ultrafast laser pulses drive non-equilibrium magnon currents in iron films. It identifies a superdiffusive transport regime that produces a spin Seebeck effect much larger than previously predicted. This work provides a way to model complex, non-thermal transport processes in magnetic materials.
main_problem: Understanding the microscopic origin of ultrafast, non-equilibrium magnon transport and demagnetization in ferromagnetic metals beyond traditional diffusive models.
main_result: The discovery of a huge ultrafast spin Seebeck effect driven by a superdiffusive regime where magnon transport transitions from ballistic to diffusive.
method: An ab initio-parameterized microscopic framework using the quantum Boltzmann equation to capture non-thermal magnon scattering and transport.
model_or_system: Bcc Iron (Fe) thin films subjected to sub-picosecond laser excitation, modeled using an extended (N+2)-temperature model that incorporates non-thermal magnon populations.
key_observables: Magneto-optical Kerr angles (rotation and ellipticity), magnetization-versus-time curves, magnon spin current density, and transport exponent (alpha).
important_parameters: Sub-picosecond/femtosecond timescales, superdiffusive regime (1 < alpha < 2), and laser fluence (1 to 4 mJ/cm2).
main_assumptions: Electrons and phonons are assumed to be internally thermalized; direct magnon-phonon coupling is neglected; higher-order magnon-magnon scattering is neglected.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the local and nonlocal (N+2)TM frameworks; Figure 2 shows temporal evolution of temperature profiles and heat currents; Figure 3 shows the magnonic spin current color map; Figure 4 shows the transport exponent evolution; Figure 6 compares simulation to experimental data and shows MOKE signals.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the limitations of the 3TM, presents the new (N+2)TM quantum Boltzmann framework, details the numerical implementation, analyzes temperature and magnetization dynamics, characterizes the superdiffusive transport regime, and validates the model against experimental MOKE data.
why_interesting: The study provides a sophisticated treatment of non-equilibrium transport and the transition from ballistic to diffusive dynamics, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and non-equilibrium many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Illustration of the method. (a) In the local (N+2)TM, the laser pulse P impinges on a metal film and heats the thermal electronic system Te homogeneously, which, in turn, is coupled to a thermal phonon bath Tp and nonther- mal magnon occupations nq. (b) The nonlocal (N+2)TM allows for a heterogeneous laser absorption profile resulting in temperature gradients. Transport occurs within each sub- system across the isolated film domain z ∈[0, L], and the subsystems exchange heat locally.

Figure 2. Nonequilibrium electron, phonon, and magnon dynamics of a laser-excited bcc Fe(001) film of thickness L = 1 µm. (a)-(f) Temperature distributions at various times. Tq± denote the temperatures of the magnon modes at q± = 2π

Figure 3. Laser-excited magnonic spin currents in a 1 µm film. (a) Magnonic spin current density following irradiation with a 1 mJ/cm2 laser pulse. Note that the time axis is piecewise linear with a breakpoint at 10 ps and only the relevant first 200 nm are shown. The dashed green line from the origin to (6 ps, 200 nm) is a guide for the eye to identify the ballistic motion of the initial burst of magnons. (b) Magnonic spin transfer across every depth during the first 10 ps for fluences from 1 mJ/cm2 to 4 mJ/cm2.

Figure 4. Transition from ballistic to diffusive transport of magnons generated at the irradiated surface during the peak of the laser pulse. (a) The instantaneous transport exponent α as a function of elapsed time. (b) The instantaneous transport exponent as a function of the average propagated distance (root mean squared displacement).

Figure 5. Calculated demagnetization dynamics. (a) Depth-resolved relative change in magnetization for a 100 nm film. Note that the time axis is piecewise linear with a breakpoint at 5 ps, allowing both the short- and long-term dynamics to be seen clearly. (b) Comparison of demagnetization curves with various transport contributions included (L = 1 µm). Each color region depicts the range of demagnetization in z ∈[0, 40] nm.
